topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glow of my phone screen illuminated my face in the dim light of the library. I was supposed to be reviewing calculus notes, but my thumb kept drifting toward the familiar profile picture of Mr. Harrison. A sudden, sharp jolt ran through my chest, making my breath catch. I hadn't seen him online in weeks; his presence on Instagram felt like an intrusion. I quickly minimized the app, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to steady my breathing. Why was he there? I scanned the screen again, a prickle of unease crawling up my neck. It was just a picture of him smiling, but the casual familiarity of it felt profoundly wrong. I closed the book with a decisive snap, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet space.

[story 2]

I was walking home, the evening air cool against my skin. My pace quickened unconsciously, my shoulders tensing as I navigated the familiar streets. Then, my phone buzzed—a notification from a platform I hadn't checked in ages. My heart gave a sudden, heavy thump against my ribs. I pulled it out, my gaze locking onto the notification. It was a post from Professor Davies, a candid photo from a recent school event. A strange tightness seized my throat. I stopped under a flickering streetlight, my hand hovering over the screen. Every instinct screamed at me to walk away, but I couldn't look away. The image was innocuous, yet the context—the way he framed the shot, the subtle caption—sent a cascade of cold anxiety through me.

[story 3]

The classroom felt unusually stifling today. I kept glancing toward the door, my focus fractured. My pen hovered over the blank page of my essay, the words refusing to form. It wasn't the lecture that was distracting; it was the persistent, nagging feeling that someone was watching. I risked a glance at my phone tucked under my desk. There, on my feed, was a story update from Ms. Evans. A brief video clip, seemingly unrelated, but the way she held her phone, the slight tilt of her head—it felt deliberate. My jaw tightened. I consciously forced my eyes back to the textbook, gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white. The silence in the room amplified the frantic rhythm of my pulse.
